questions.explanation: Allah created seven heavens, as mentioned in the Quran (Surah Al-Mulk 67:3), each layered above the other, demonstrating His vastness and the order in creation. This concept highlights the structured nature of the universe in Islamic belief.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Throne & The Kursi
questions.q: What is the "Bayt al-Ma'mur" (Much-Frequented House), and where is it located?
opts: In the first heaven, directly above the Kaaba
In the seventh heaven, directly above the Kaaba, where 70,000 angels enter daily and never return
questions.explanation: Bayt al-Ma'mur is a celestial house in the seventh heaven, positioned directly above the Kaaba on Earth. It is visited daily by 70,000 angels who enter but never return, symbolizing a parallel place of worship in the heavens.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Throne & The Kursi
questions.q: What linguistic point does Ibn Kathir make about the conjunction "waw" (and) in verses about creation?
opts: It does not necessarily imply order; it is used for absolute conjunction
It proves the earth was created before the heavens
questions.explanation: Ibn Kathir explains that the conjunction "waw" (and) in creation verses does not always indicate a sequence of events but serves as an absolute conjunction, linking ideas without implying chronological order. This highlights that the order mentioned may not reflect the actual order of creation.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Throne & The Kursi
questions.q: According to the hadith of Abu Dharr, what fills every span of four fingers in the heavens?
questions.explanation: In the hadith of Abu Dharr, the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) describes that every span of four fingers in the heavens is filled with angels prostrating to Allah, illustrating their continuous worship and submission to Him throughout the vastness of the heavens.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Throne & The Kursi
questions.q: What was the span of Jibril's wings according to the hadith of Ibn Mas'ud?
opts: Each wing covered the distance between east and west
Each wing covered the entire earth
Each wing reached from the earth to the Throne
Each wing was the size of the seven heavens
questions.explanation: According to the hadith narrated by Ibn Mas'ud, Jibril's wings were so vast that each one stretched from the east to the west, illustrating the immense power and majesty of this angel in Islamic tradition. This emphasizes the grandeur of the unseen world described in hadith literature.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Throne & The Kursi
questions.q: What is the significance of the seventy thousand angels who enter the Bayt al-Ma'mur daily?
opts: They are the only angels in the seventh heaven
They enter and never return, illustrating the infinite vastness of Allah's creation and armies
They are responsible for bringing rain
They are the angels who prostrated to Adam
questions.explanation: The seventy thousand angels who enter the Bayt al-Ma'mur daily and never return demonstrate the immense scale of Allah’s creation and His countless heavenly armies, highlighting His infinite power and majesty beyond human comprehension.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Throne & The Kursi
questions.q: What is the name of the angel who is the keeper of the Bayt al-Ma'mur in the lowest heaven?
questions.explanation: The angel who is the keeper of the Bayt al-Ma'mur in the lowest heaven is not Ishmael (Isma'il), but rather an angel specifically appointed for this role. Ishmael is a prophet, not an angel, and the Bayt al-Ma'mur is visited by many angels daily, each entering once and never returning.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Throne & The Kursi
questions.q: According to the poetry of Al-Farazdaq cited by Ibn Kathir, what did he say about the Bayt al-Ma'mur?
opts: "The One who raised the heavens built for us a house whose foundations are stronger and taller"
"The Bayt al-Ma'mur is the Kaaba of the angels"
questions.explanation: Al-Farazdaq’s poetry highlights the Bayt al-Ma'mur as a divine structure created by Allah, emphasizing its strength and height to symbolize its significance and permanence in the heavens, reflecting Allah’s power in building a house more enduring than the earthly realm.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Jinn & Iblis
questions.q: What happens to disbelieving Jinn on the Day of Judgment?
opts: They are forgiven because they were not sent prophets
questions.explanation: Disbelieving Jinn, like disbelieving humans, are held accountable for their rejection of Allah's guidance and are punished in the Hereafter. On the Day of Judgment, they will enter Hellfire alongside Iblis as a consequence of their disbelief and rebellion against Allah.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of Adam ﷺ
questions.q: Who ate from the tree first, according to many narrations?
questions.explanation: Many narrations state that Hawwa (Eve) ate from the tree first, which led to both her and Adam ﷺ being tested. This highlights the human aspect of their experience and the beginning of their repentance and guidance from Allah.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Pen & The Tablet
questions.q: What words of repentance did Adam learn from his Lord?
opts: "Rabbana zalamna anfusana wa in lam taghfir lana wa tarhamna lanakunanna min al-khasireen"
"La ilaha illa anta subhanaka inni kuntu min al-thalimeen"
questions.explanation: Adam was taught the words of repentance, "Rabbana zalamna anfusana wa in lam taghfir lana wa tarhamna lanakunanna min al-khasireen," meaning "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers," showing sincere acknowledgment of sin and seeking Allah’s mercy.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Pen & The Tablet
questions.q: Did Allah accept Adam's repentance?
opts: Yes, Allah accepted his repentance and guided him
After Adam was sent to earth
questions.explanation: Allah accepted Adam's repentance because He is Most Merciful and Forgiving. After Adam and Eve disobeyed, they sincerely sought forgiveness, and Allah guided them, demonstrating His mercy and the importance of turning back to Him after mistakes.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What happened to the leaves of Paradise that came down with Adam?
opts: They were planted and from them grew fragrant plants and trees
They were taken back to heaven
questions.explanation: The leaves of Paradise that came down with Adam were planted on Earth, and from them grew fragrant plants and trees, symbolizing the transfer of divine blessings from Paradise to the earthly realm. This illustrates how elements of Paradise were integrated into the natural world for humanity's benefit.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Throne & The Kursi
questions.q: What does the Quranic phrase "wa rafa'nahu makaanan 'aliyya" (and We raised him to a high station) mean according to Ibn Kathir?
opts: He was taken up to the heavens and is alive
questions.explanation: Ibn Kathir explains that "wa rafa'nahu makaanan 'aliyya" means Prophet Isa (Jesus) was physically raised up to the heavens by Allah, where he remains alive, rather than being killed, highlighting his special status and divine protection.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What was the sign that the flood was about to begin?
questions.explanation: Before the flood began, water gushing from the oven (tannur) was a clear sign indicating the impending deluge. This event symbolized the release of overwhelming water, marking the start of the flood as described in Islamic tradition.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: Where did the ark come to rest after the flood?
questions.explanation: The ark came to rest on Mount Judi after the flood, as mentioned in Islamic tradition and the Quran (Surah Hud 11:44). This location is distinct from the biblical Mount Ararat and signifies where Prophet Noah's mission concluded successfully.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What happened to the earth after the flood?
opts: Allah commanded the earth to swallow the water and the sky to cease raining
questions.explanation: After the flood, Allah commanded the earth to absorb the excess water and the sky to stop raining, which caused the floodwaters to recede and allowed life to begin anew on the earth's surface. This demonstrates Allah's control over natural elements in the process of creation and renewal.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What were the instructions regarding the she-camel?
opts: To let it drink from the water one day and the people the next day, and not to harm it
To keep it in a temple
questions.explanation: The she-camel was a sign from Allah to the people of Thamud, and they were instructed to share the water by letting the camel drink one day and the people the next, ensuring no harm came to it as a test of their obedience and respect for divine commands.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Pen & The Tablet
questions.q: When did the punishment come?
opts: After three days, during which they awaited it
questions.explanation: The punishment came after three days because the people were given a period to reflect and repent, but when they persisted in their wrongdoing, the punishment was then executed as a consequence of their actions. This waiting period highlights divine justice and mercy before enforcing punishment.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: How did Isma'il respond when his father told him about the command to sacrifice him?
opts: He said, "O my father, do as you are commanded; you will find me, if Allah wills, among the steadfast"
questions.explanation: Isma'il showed complete submission and trust in Allah by encouraging his father to fulfill the command, expressing his own readiness and faith that he would be patient and steadfast through the trial. This demonstrates the importance of obedience and trust in God's will in Islam.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Throne & The Kursi
questions.q: What is the name of the "Much-Frequented House" (Al-Bayt al-Ma'mur) and in which heaven is it located?
opts: In the first heaven, directly above the Kaaba
In the seventh heaven, directly above the Kaaba
In the second heaven, where the angels worship
In the fourth heaven, visited by Prophet Idris
questions.explanation: Al-Bayt al-Ma'mur, the "Much-Frequented House," is a celestial house of worship located in the seventh heaven, directly above the Kaaba on Earth. It is visited by angels who worship there, and each day it is frequented by a multitude of angels, never to be visited again by the same ones.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Angels
questions.q: What is the name of the angel who visited Prophet Ibrahim (Abraham) when the people of Lut (Lot) were about to be destroyed?
opts: Israfil with a host of angels
Ridwan with the keys to punishment
questions.explanation: Prophet Ibrahim (Abraham) was visited by the angel Jibril along with two other angels who came to inform him about the impending destruction of the people of Lut (Lot). These angels carried out Allah’s command to punish the wicked inhabitants for their sins.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Angels
questions.q: How did these angels appear to Prophet Lut when they visited him?
questions.explanation: The angels appeared to Prophet Lut as handsome young men to blend in with the people of the town and to test their behavior without revealing their true nature. This form helped convey their message and mission discreetly.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Pen & The Tablet
questions.q: According to the hadith about Adam and Musa (Moses) meeting, why did Adam defeat Musa in their argument?
opts: Adam had more knowledge of the Torah
Adam argued that his sin was predestined by Allah before he was created, and blaming him was like blaming Allah for His own decree
Musa was not a prophet at the time of the debate
Adam had already repented, so his sin was erased
questions.explanation: Adam defeated Musa because he explained that his sin was already decreed by Allah before his creation, so holding him accountable would mean blaming Allah's divine will, which is beyond human control. This highlights the concept of predestination in Islamic theology.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of Adam ﷺ
questions.q: What items came down with Adam from Paradise, according to some narrations?
opts: A tent, a staff, and a sword
The Black Stone, a handful of Paradise's leaves, and knowledge of all trades
questions.explanation: According to some narrations, Adam descended from Paradise carrying the Black Stone, a handful of Paradise's leaves, and knowledge of all trades, symbolizing divine blessings and wisdom granted to him for guidance and sustenance on Earth.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Adam's Descent & Early Humanity
questions.q: What was Qabil's reaction when his sacrifice was not accepted?
opts: He became jealous and angry, threatening to kill Habil
questions.explanation: Qabil's sacrifice was rejected by Allah, which made him jealous and angry because Habil's offering was accepted. This jealousy led Qabil to threaten and eventually kill Habil, marking the first murder in human history according to Islamic tradition.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Pen & The Tablet
questions.q: What happened to humanity between the time of Adam and Nuh (Noah)?
opts: They gradually fell into corruption and idolatry until Allah sent Noah
questions.explanation: Between the time of Adam and Nuh, humanity initially followed Allah's guidance but gradually deviated by engaging in corruption and idol worship. This moral decline prompted Allah to send Prophet Nuh to call people back to monotheism and righteousness.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of Adam ﷺ
questions.q: Who was Prophet Idris (Enoch) in relation to Adam and Nuh?
opts: He was a son of Adam
He was a descendant of Adam and an ancestor of Nuh (Noah)
He was a contemporary of Adam
questions.explanation: Prophet Idris (Enoch) was a descendant of Prophet Adam and lived many generations before Prophet Nuh (Noah), making him an ancestor of Nuh. This lineage shows the continuity of prophethood and guidance through successive generations.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What was Prophet Idris known for according to the Quran and narrations?
opts: He was a truthful prophet, a man of truth, and was raised to a high station
questions.explanation: Prophet Idris is described in the Quran as a truthful and patient prophet who was honored by being raised to a high station by Allah. His exemplary character and devotion make him a significant figure in Islamic teachings.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Throne & The Kursi
questions.q: In which heaven did Prophet Muhammad ﷺ meet Idris during the Isra and Mi'raj?
questions.explanation: Prophet Muhammad ﷺ met Prophet Idris in the fourth heaven during the Isra and Mi'raj journey. This meeting signifies the honor given to Idris, who is known for his wisdom and closeness to Allah, highlighting the spiritual hierarchy among prophets in Islamic tradition.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Pen & The Tablet
questions.q: What profession is Prophet Idris associated with in some narrations?
opts: Tailoring - he was the first to sew garments with needle and thread
questions.explanation: Prophet Idris is associated with tailoring because some narrations describe him as the first person to sew clothes using a needle and thread, marking an important advancement in human craftsmanship and civilization.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of Adam ﷺ
questions.q: How many prophets are mentioned between Adam and Nuh according to Ibn Kathir's compilation?
opts: Idris is the only one mentioned specifically
questions.explanation: Between Adam and Nuh, Ibn Kathir specifically mentions only Prophet Idris as a prophet. Other figures are not explicitly named as prophets in his compilation, highlighting Idris's unique mention in this early period of prophetic history.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of Adam ﷺ
questions.q: What was the condition of humanity between the time of Adam and Nuh?
opts: They gradually fell into corruption and idolatry until Allah sent Nuh
questions.explanation: After Adam, humanity initially followed monotheism but over generations gradually deviated by engaging in corruption and idol worship, leading to widespread moral decline. This prompted Allah to send Prophet Nuh to guide them back to the right path.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of Adam ﷺ
questions.q: Why is Nuh (Noah) considered the first "Messenger" (Rasul) while Adam was a Prophet (Nabi)?
opts: Because Nuh was greater than Adam
Because Nuh was sent to a disbelieving people with a new law, while Adam taught his children the original religion
questions.explanation: Nuh is considered the first Messenger because he was sent with a new divine law to guide a disbelieving community, whereas Adam, as the first Prophet, taught his descendants the original, primordial religion without introducing a new legal code.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: How long did Prophet Nuh call his people to Islam?
questions.explanation: Prophet Nuh called his people to Islam for 950 years, demonstrating his patience and perseverance in inviting them to monotheism despite their persistent rejection. This long duration highlights the importance of steadfastness in dawah (inviting others to faith).

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of Adam ﷺ
questions.q: What was the response of Nuh's people to his preaching?
opts: They put their fingers in their ears, covered themselves with their garments, persisted in disbelief, and were arrogant
questions.explanation: Nuh's people rejected his message by deliberately blocking out his words, showing their stubbornness and arrogance. This behavior demonstrated their refusal to listen or accept guidance, leading to their persistent disbelief despite his warnings.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Adam's Descent & Early Humanity
questions.q: Who were the leaders of disbelief among Nuh's people?
opts: Wadd, Suwa', Yaghuth, Ya'uq, and Nasr - originally righteous men whose images were worshipped
questions.explanation: Wadd, Suwa', Yaghuth, Ya'uq, and Nasr were originally righteous men from Prophet Nuh's people whose images became objects of worship, leading them to become leaders of disbelief and idolatry among their people.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What did Nuh build by Allah's command?
questions.explanation: Nuh (Noah) built an ark by Allah's command to save his family and pairs of animals from the great flood. This ark was a large ship designed to survive the floodwaters and preserve life, demonstrating obedience and trust in Allah’s guidance.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: Where did Nuh build the ark?
opts: In the desert far from water, to show the power of Allah
questions.explanation: Nuh built the ark in a dry desert area away from water to demonstrate that the flood and the ark's survival were miracles from Allah, not ordinary events. This showed Allah's power to create and control nature beyond human expectations.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: How did the people react when they saw Nuh building the ark?
opts: They mocked him and laughed at him
questions.explanation: People mocked and laughed at Nuh because building an ark on dry land seemed illogical and strange to them, especially since there was no sign of an impending flood. Their disbelief in his warning about the coming flood led them to ridicule his efforts.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of Adam ﷺ
questions.q: How many people believed with Nuh?
opts: Only a few - his family except one son, and some others
questions.explanation: Only a small number of people believed in Nuh’s message, including his family except one son who rejected him, and a few others who accepted the faith. This highlights the widespread rejection Nuh faced during his prophetic mission.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of Adam ﷺ
questions.q: What did Nuh take with him on the ark?
opts: Pairs of every animal species, and the believers
questions.explanation: Nuh (Noah) took pairs of every animal species on the ark to preserve life after the flood, along with the believers who followed him, ensuring the continuation of humanity and animal life as described in Islamic teachings.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of Adam ﷺ
questions.q: Who was the son of Nuh who did not believe and was drowned?
opts: Canaan (or Yam) - his name is given in some narrations
questions.explanation: Nuh’s son who did not believe and was drowned in the flood is named Canaan or Yam in some narrations. Despite being the son of a prophet, he rejected the message, illustrating that faith is a personal choice regardless of lineage.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What did Nuh say to his son when he refused to board the ark?
opts: "There is no protector today from the decree of Allah except those on whom He has mercy"
questions.explanation: Nuh told his son that no one can escape Allah’s decree except those whom Allah shows mercy, emphasizing that divine will is unavoidable and protection comes only through Allah’s mercy, not through personal choice or defiance.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What did Nuh ask Allah regarding his drowned son?
opts: "My Lord, indeed my son is of my family"
questions.explanation: Nuh asked Allah to consider his son as part of his family, hoping for his salvation despite his son's disbelief. This highlights the human desire for mercy and the importance of faith in Allah's judgment during trials.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of Adam ﷺ
questions.q: What was Allah's response to Nuh?
opts: "He was not of your family; he was of unrighteous conduct"
"He will be resurrected as a believer"
"Do not ask Me for what you have no knowledge"
questions.explanation: Allah informed Nuh that the person he was concerned about was not truly part of his righteous family but was characterized by unrighteous behavior, emphasizing that spiritual kinship is based on faith and conduct rather than mere lineage.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: Who was Prophet Hud (peace be upon him) and to which people was he sent?
opts: To the people of 'Ad in Al-Ahqaf (the sand dunes) in Yemen
questions.explanation: Prophet Hud (peace be upon him) was sent as a messenger to the people of 'Ad, who lived in the region of Al-Ahqaf, known for its sand dunes in Yemen. He called them to worship Allah alone and abandon idolatry, but they largely rejected his message.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What was the people of 'Ad known for?
opts: Their physical strength and tall stature, and their construction of buildings with lofty pillars
questions.explanation: The people of 'Ad were renowned for their exceptional physical strength and towering height, which distinguished them from others. They also built impressive structures with lofty pillars, showcasing their advanced architectural skills and power.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What was the main sin of the people of 'Ad?
opts: They were arrogant and said "Who is stronger than us?"
questions.explanation: The people of 'Ad were known for their arrogance, idolatry, and rejection of Prophet Hud's message, which collectively constituted their main sin. Their pride and disobedience led to their destruction as a warning to others.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What message did Hud preach to his people?
opts: "Worship Allah alone; you have no god other than Him"
questions.explanation: Hud preached to his people the message of monotheism, urging them to worship Allah alone and reject all forms of idolatry, emphasizing that there is no god besides Him. This was central to his mission to guide them back to the true faith.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of Adam ﷺ
questions.q: How did the people of 'Ad respond to Hud?
opts: They accused him of foolishness, lying, and said, "You have only brought us evil"
questions.explanation: The people of 'Ad rejected Prophet Hud's message by calling him foolish and a liar because they refused to abandon their idol worship and sinful ways, viewing his warnings as harmful rather than guidance from Allah.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What punishment did Allah send upon the people of 'Ad?
opts: A fierce, screaming, cold wind that destroyed everything for seven nights and eight days
questions.explanation: Allah sent a powerful, cold, and destructive wind that lasted seven nights and eight days to punish the people of 'Ad for their disobedience, completely devastating their land and people as a consequence of their rejection of His guidance.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Pen & The Tablet
questions.q: What happened to the wind when it struck the people of 'Ad?
opts: It threw them down dead like uprooted palm trunks
questions.explanation: The wind sent upon the people of 'Ad was a severe, divine punishment that struck them with such force it uprooted and killed them like palm trunks being torn from the ground, demonstrating the power of Allah's wrath against their disobedience.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of Adam ﷺ
questions.q: Who was saved from the punishment?
opts: Hud and those who believed with him
questions.explanation: Hud was a prophet sent to the people of 'Ad, and those who believed in his message were saved from the punishment that befell the disbelievers. Their faith and obedience protected them from the divine punishment described in Islamic teachings.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: Who was Prophet Salih (peace be upon him) and to which people was he sent?
opts: To the people of Thamud in Al-Hijr (Mada'in Salih)
questions.explanation: Prophet Salih (peace be upon him) was sent by Allah to the people of Thamud, who lived in the region of Al-Hijr (Mada'in Salih). He called them to worship Allah alone and warned them against their wrongdoing, but most rejected his message.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What was the people of Thamud known for?
opts: Their skill in carving homes out of mountains
questions.explanation: The people of Thamud were renowned for their exceptional ability to carve elaborate homes and structures directly into the mountains, showcasing advanced craftsmanship and architectural skills in a harsh desert environment. This unique talent distinguished them in historical and Islamic narratives.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What miracle did Allah give to the people of Thamud through Salih?
opts: A staff that turned into a snake
A she-camel that emerged from a rock, with specific instructions not to harm her
questions.explanation: Allah granted the people of Thamud a miraculous she-camel that emerged from a rock as a clear sign of His power through Prophet Salih. They were instructed not to harm the camel, symbolizing obedience and faith, but their disobedience led to their punishment.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of Adam ﷺ
questions.q: How did the people of Thamud respond to Salih's message?
opts: They asked for the miracle, then after it came, most disbelieved and demanded Salih bring the punishment
They accepted the camel as their god
questions.explanation: The people of Thamud challenged Prophet Salih to show a miracle as proof of his prophethood. When the she-camel appeared as a sign, many still rejected the message and eventually demanded the punishment, leading to their destruction.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of Adam ﷺ
questions.q: Who killed the she-camel?
opts: A man named Qudar ibn Salif, with the support of nine other evildoers
questions.explanation: Qudar ibn Salif, along with nine accomplices, killed the she-camel sent as a sign by Prophet Salih ﷺ to the people of Thamud. This act of defiance led to their destruction, illustrating the consequences of rejecting divine signs and disobeying God's commands.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Throne & The Kursi
questions.q: What punishment did Allah send upon the people of Thamud after they killed the camel?
opts: A single great blast (sayhah) that destroyed them
questions.explanation: After the people of Thamud killed the miraculous camel sent as a sign, Allah punished them with a single great blast (sayhah) that destroyed them instantly, demonstrating His swift and decisive justice against their disobedience and cruelty.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What is Prophet Ibrahim's title?
questions.explanation: Prophet Ibrahim is called Khalilullah because he had a close, intimate friendship with Allah, marked by his unwavering faith and obedience. This title highlights his special status among prophets as the beloved friend of God.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What was the occupation of Ibrahim's father (or uncle) Azar?
opts: A maker and seller of idols
questions.explanation: Azar, the father or uncle of Prophet Ibrahim (peace be upon him), was known for making and selling idols, which was a common practice in his community before the advent of monotheism. This background highlights Ibrahim's challenge in calling people to worship one God alone.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: How did Ibrahim first reason against idol worship?
opts: By observing the stars, moon, and sun, realizing they set and are not worthy of worship
questions.explanation: Ibrahim observed that stars, the moon, and the sun all rise and set, showing they are not eternal or all-powerful, so they cannot be true gods. This reasoning led him to reject idol worship and believe in one eternal Creator.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What did Ibrahim do to the idols in the temple?
opts: He broke them all except the largest, and placed the axe on the largest idol
questions.explanation: Ibrahim broke all the idols in the temple except the largest one to demonstrate that they had no power to protect themselves, then placed the axe on the biggest idol to show that it was not responsible for the destruction, highlighting the futility of idol worship.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Angels
questions.q: When the people asked Ibrahim who did this, what did he say?
opts: "Ask the largest idol; if he can speak"
questions.explanation: Ibrahim responded this way to show the foolishness of idol worship, pointing out that idols are lifeless and cannot speak or act, so they cannot be responsible for anything. This highlighted the need to worship the one true God who has power and knowledge.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Jinn & Iblis
questions.q: How did the people punish Ibrahim?
opts: They threw him into a great fire
questions.explanation: Ibrahim was punished by being thrown into a great fire because he rejected the idol worship of his people and called them to monotheism. However, Allah miraculously saved him by making the fire cool and harmless, demonstrating His power and protection for His prophets.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Jinn & Iblis
questions.q: What did Allah command the fire to do?
opts: To be cool and safe for Ibrahim
questions.explanation: Allah commanded the fire to be cool and safe for Prophet Ibrahim so that it would not harm him when he was thrown into it by his people, demonstrating Allah’s power to protect His prophets from harm.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Angels
questions.q: Which angel offered to help Ibrahim in the fire?
questions.explanation: Angel Jibril (Gabriel) is known for assisting Prophet Ibrahim when he was thrown into the fire by his people, by bringing down coolness and safety, demonstrating Allah’s protection and support for His prophets.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Angels
questions.q: How did Ibrahim respond to the angel's offer?
opts: "As for you, no; as for Allah, yes" meaning his trust was in Allah alone
questions.explanation: Ibrahim's response, "As for you, no; as for Allah, yes," shows his complete trust and reliance on Allah alone, rejecting any offer from the angel that might compromise his faith. This highlights his unwavering devotion and submission to God's will.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: Who was Ibrahim's wife who accompanied him on his journeys?
opts: Both a and b - Sarah was his first wife, Hajirah was the second
questions.explanation: Ibrahim's first wife was Sarah, who accompanied him during the early part of his life, while Hajirah (Hagar) was his second wife and also traveled with him, especially during the time they settled in different places as part of Allah's plan. Both played significant roles in his story.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What was the name of Ibrahim's first son, born from Hajirah?
questions.explanation: Ibrahim's first son, born to Hajirah (Hagar), was Isma'il (Ishmael). He is an important prophet in Islam and is considered the ancestor of many Arab tribes.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What was the name of Ibrahim's second son, born from Sarah?
questions.explanation: Ibrahim's second son, born to Sarah, was named Ishaq (Isaac). He is an important prophet in Islam, known for his righteousness and as the father of Yaqub (Jacob).

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: Where did Ibrahim settle Isma'il and Hajirah by Allah's command?
opts: In the valley of Makkah (Bakkah) by the Sacred House
questions.explanation: Ibrahim settled Isma'il and Hajirah in the valley of Makkah (Bakkah) near the Sacred House (Kaaba) by Allah's command to establish a place for worship and to fulfill divine plans, marking the beginning of Makkah as a significant spiritual center in Islam.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What did Allah command Ibrahim to do in a vision regarding his son?
questions.explanation: Allah commanded Prophet Ibrahim in a vision to sacrifice his son as a test of faith and obedience. This event symbolizes submission to Allah's will and is commemorated annually during Eid al-Adha.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: Which son was it that Ibrahim was commanded to sacrifice according to the most authentic Islamic view?
opts: It is disputed, but the majority hold it was Isma'il
questions.explanation: Ibrahim was commanded to sacrifice his son Isma'il according to the most authentic Islamic view, as supported by Quranic verses and Hadith. This event symbolizes submission to Allah's will and is commemorated during Eid al-Adha.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Pen & The Tablet
questions.q: What happened when Ibrahim attempted to sacrifice his son?
opts: Allah ransomed him with a great sacrifice (a ram from Paradise)
Both b and c - the knife would not cut, and then a ram was provided
questions.explanation: When Ibrahim attempted to sacrifice his son, the knife would not cut, showing divine intervention, and then Allah provided a ram to be sacrificed instead, demonstrating obedience and God's mercy.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What did Ibrahim and Isma'il build together?
opts: The foundations of the Kaaba (the Sacred House)
questions.explanation: Ibrahim (Abraham) and his son Isma'il (Ishmael) built the foundations of the Kaaba, the sacred house in Mecca, as a place of monotheistic worship dedicated to Allah. This act established the Kaaba as the central site for Muslim prayer and pilgrimage.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: Who was Prophet Lut (Lot) in relation to Ibrahim?
questions.explanation: Prophet Lut (Lot) was the nephew of Prophet Ibrahim (Abraham), meaning he was the son of Ibrahim’s brother. This familial relationship is mentioned in Islamic tradition and the Qur'an, highlighting their close kinship and shared prophetic missions.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: Where was Lut sent as a prophet?
opts: To the cities of the plain (Sodom and Gomorrah), known for their wickedness
questions.explanation: Lut was sent as a prophet to the cities of the plain, specifically Sodom and Gomorrah, which were known for their immoral behavior and corruption. His mission was to call the people to repentance and warn them against their sinful ways.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What was the primary sin of the people of Lut?
opts: Approaching males with desire instead of females (homosexuality)
questions.explanation: The primary sin of the people of Lut was engaging in homosexual acts, which involved approaching males with desire instead of females. This behavior was condemned in Islamic teachings as it violated the natural order and moral guidelines set by Allah.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Angels
questions.q: Who visited Lut in the form of handsome young men?
questions.explanation: Lut was visited by three angels who appeared as handsome young men to test the people of Sodom and Gomorrah. One of these angels was Jibril (Gabriel), accompanied by two others, sent by Allah to deliver the message and carry out His command.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What did the wicked people of the city demand when they heard about Lut's guests?
questions.explanation: The wicked people of the city demanded to seize Prophet Lut's guests to commit immoral acts, showing their rejection of righteousness and hospitality. This behavior highlights their corruption and was a key reason for their eventual punishment.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: How were the people of Lut destroyed?
opts: By an earthquake, and then Allah rained upon them stones of baked clay, one after another
questions.explanation: The people of Lut were destroyed as a punishment for their wrongdoing; first by a severe earthquake that shook their land, followed by a rain of stones of baked clay, which caused complete devastation, as described in Islamic teachings and the Quran.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: Who was Prophet Yusuf (Joseph) and who was his father?
questions.explanation: Prophet Yusuf (Joseph) was a prophet known for his wisdom and patience, and he was the son of Prophet Ya'qub (Jacob). Ya'qub is also a prophet, and Yusuf’s story is detailed in the Quran, highlighting themes of faith, forgiveness, and divine guidance.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of Adam ﷺ
questions.q: What did Yusuf see in his dream that he told his father?
opts: Eleven stars, the sun, and the moon prostrating to him
He was thrown in a well
questions.explanation: Yusuf saw eleven stars, the sun, and the moon prostrating to him in his dream, symbolizing his eleven brothers, his father, and his mother showing respect and submission. This dream indicated his future elevated status and the eventual reconciliation within his family.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: Why did Yusuf's brothers hate him?
opts: Because their father loved him more, and they were jealous
Because he was mean to them
questions.explanation: Yusuf's brothers hated him because their father, Prophet Ya'qub (Jacob), showed him special love and favoritism, which made them feel jealous and neglected. This jealousy led them to mistreat Yusuf and plot against him.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What did Yusuf's brothers do to him?
opts: They threw him into a well and told their father a wolf ate him
They sold him to a trader
They kept him as a servant
questions.explanation: Yusuf's brothers, out of jealousy, threw him into a well to get rid of him and then lied to their father, saying a wolf had eaten him, to hide their wrongdoing and avoid punishment. This story highlights themes of envy and deception in early Islamic history.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: Who found Yusuf in the well and took him?
questions.explanation: Yusuf was found in the well by a passing caravan, who then took him out and sold him as a slave in Egypt. This event is significant as it marks the beginning of Yusuf's journey from hardship to eventual rise in status.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: Where did Yusuf end up after being taken from the well?
opts: In Egypt, in the house of Al-Aziz (the chief minister)
questions.explanation: Yusuf was found by a caravan and taken to Egypt, where he was sold to Al-Aziz, the chief minister. This marked the beginning of his significant role in Egyptian society, as described in Islamic tradition and the story in Al-Bidaya wal-Nihaya.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · The Pen & The Tablet
questions.q: What happened between Yusuf and the wife of Al-Aziz?
opts: She tried to seduce him, but he refused and sought refuge with Allah
questions.explanation: Yusuf (Joseph) was tested by the wife of Al-Aziz when she attempted to seduce him, but he remained steadfast and sought protection from Allah, demonstrating his strong faith and moral integrity. This event highlights the importance of resisting temptation and trusting in Allah's help.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: What evidence proved Yusuf's innocence when the women of the city gossiped?
opts: His shirt was torn from the back, indicating he was fleeing
The women cut their hands when they saw his beauty
questions.explanation: Yusuf's innocence was proven when the women's fingers were cut after their false accusation, and the truth came to light through the testimony of a witness and the discovery of Yusuf's shirt, which showed signs of struggle, confirming he was not guilty of the alleged wrongdoing.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Creation of the Earth
questions.q: Where was Prophet Ibrahim born and raised?
opts: In Ur of the Chaldeans (in modern-day Iraq), among a people who worshipped idols
questions.explanation: Prophet Ibrahim was born and raised in Ur of the Chaldeans, an ancient city in present-day Iraq, where people practiced idol worship. This context highlights his mission to call people to monotheism and reject idolatry.

questions.topic: Beginning of Time · Angels
questions.q: What happened to Hajirah when she ran out of water for baby Isma'il?
opts: She ran between Safa and Marwa seven times searching for water, and Allah caused the Zamzam well to spring forth
questions.explanation: When Hajirah ran out of water for baby Isma'il, she desperately searched by running between the hills of Safa and Marwa seven times. Allah then caused the Zamzam well to spring forth, providing water and saving her child. This event is commemorated during the Hajj pilgrimage.
